conclusion: Morita rebuilt Sony from burned-out Tokyo rubble with co-founder Ibuka — high-end quality when Japan meant cheap copycats, transistor radios sold through demos, and Walkman shipped despite internal revolt to 400 million units. Senra links him to Jobs' turtleneck, Bezos' customer-centric mission, and Land's premium innovation: create markets, educate consumers, refuse committee consensus.
background: Senra rereads Made in Japan (1986) after We Were Burning and founder cross-references from Bezos and Jobs. Morita, 25, gathered in a war-devastated department store; Hiroshima news struck him as proof of overwhelming American industry — Ford River Rouge at 2,000 acres terrified him. A 300-year sake heir who flunked school for electronics, he partnered with Ibuka in Telecommunications Research Laboratories, won U.S. military contracts with sand-bucket fire safety, and fought Regency to establish transistor pocket radios and Walkman culture.
important_facts: Postwar Tokyo: population under half pre-war; 100,000 killed in one firebomb night; Morita watched Ford's integrated factory films and chose electronics over food scarcity. Father nearly bankrupted 300-year brewery collecting art — Morita learned multigenerational wealth requires hands-on stewardship, not outsourced managers.

First U.S. contract: American general toured primitive shop, recommended sand buckets; delivered quality mixing units and won trust. Pocket transistor required larger shirt pockets — Morita sold fashion with hardware. Walkman: Ibuka wanted music without disturbing others; internal opposition called it mad — 400 million units sold.

Fifth Avenue showroom and direct communication replaced intermediary ignorance — Morita spoke English, immersed in America, refused to hand off brand to distributors. Neimawashi patience for consensus; mottenai shame of waste like Land and Jobs rigor. Norio Oga hired from criticizing Sony audio — ballet-dancer mirror philosophy.

mental_model.name: Create the Market You Need
mental_model.components: Morita did not ask what customers wanted — he educated them on products that did not exist, demoing to courts, stenographers, and Fifth Avenue crowds. Premium innovation with showmanship beat me-too manufacturing; miniaturization and marketing were engineering equals. Defiance under defeat — emperor's surrender speech as call to rebuild — powered confidence competitors lacked.
mental_model.application: Launch with demos to institutions that validate category need; control distribution narrative when intermediaries dilute quality. When product requires behavior change (Walkman headphones), ship anyway if insight is clear. Hire harsh critics who mirror craft standards — Oga from vocal student to president.

key_insights.question: What mission did Morita share with Bezos?
key_insights.answer: After WWII Sony would make Japan known for quality, not cheap copies — parallel to Bezos making Amazon Earth's most customer-centric company. Obsessive focus on standard-bearing products versus competitor fixation.

key_insights.question: How did Ibuka measure frequency without instruments?
key_insights.answer: With 1,500 mechanical elements he hired students with perfect pitch to check 1,000-cycle tuning forks — resourcefulness Wozniak would recognize. Shortwave adapters turned illegal radios into mass market under occupation rules.

key_insights.question: How did Morita react to Hiroshima news?
key_insights.answer: As a 25-year-old physics officer he understood the bomb meant American industrial superiority — yet felt he could still play a role rebuilding Japan. Defiance: when ordered toward mass suicide he asked who would court-martial the emperor's soldiers after surrender.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Morita's market-creation and premium-brand arc — Walkman, Trinitron era discipline, direct U.S. retail — explains Sony's IP and brand heritage even as divisions cycle; biography supports moat in premium electronics and content where innovation culture persists.

golden_quotes: Think Walkman — Dyson on Morita's balls to ship a product that doesn't record, only plays, and enlighten lives.
When the American general saw our shop he recommended buckets of sand in case of fire — we still delivered quality that marveled skeptics.
Jobs wore black turtlenecks after meeting Morita; Izzy Miyake made a hundred in his closet — uniform as brand discipline.

chronology.subject: Akio Morita · Ibuka · Sony · Made in Japan

events.event: Morita and Ibuka found Telecommunications Research Laboratories in burned Tokyo.
U.S. occupation contracts; transistor pocket radio; shirt-pocket marketing.
Walkman launches despite internal opposition; sells roughly 400 million units lifetime.
Fifth Avenue showroom; direct U.S. marketing; premium global brand building.
Made in Japan published; Morita writes at height of Sony influence.
